svg.text: TACOMA NARROWS BRIDGE — AEROELASTIC FAILURE ANALYSIS
7 NOVEMBER 1940 · TORSIONAL FLUTTER AT ~42 MPH WIND · SPAN: 2,800 FT · "GALLOPING GERTIE"
vs. 120 mph design limit (~12% of capacity)
Root cause: solid plate-girder deck acted as an airfoil generating aerodynamic lift & moment
Deck was too slender and torsionally weak. Deflection-theory design favoured economy over aerodynamic stability.
No pre-construction wind-tunnel testing was required or performed. Farquharson's retrofit had not yet been installed.
Max deck rise one side: 28 ft
DETAIL PANELS — FAILURE MECHANISM, SWISS CHEESE & 5-WHYS ROOT CAUSE
Federal funding required cheaper design. Plate girder deck chosen over open truss.
Workers note vertical oscillations. Four hydraulic buffers installed — no improvement observed.
Oct 1940 — Tie-down cables added; center span unaffected
One tie-down cable snapped Nov 1. Farquharson's retrofit (perforated girders) not yet installed.
7 Nov, ~9:00 AM — 42 mph wind, deck oscillates vertically
Frequency ~0.60 Hz. Traffic still moving. Wind-induced vertical mode — seemingly familiar.
~10:00 AM — North midspan cable band slips
Splits north cable into unequal lengths. Mode switches abruptly to torsion at ~0.23 Hz.
Deck twists 45°; 28 ft rise per side. ~700 torsional cycles. Concrete sections fall,
girders twist, bolts shear, suspenders snap. Bridge falls into Puget Sound.
c_eff(U) < 0 → θ(t) ≈ θ₀ · e^(σt) · sin(ωt), σ > 0
Negative aerodynamic damping — oscillation grows without bound
Refs: WSDOT TNB History · Cambridge J. Fluid Mech. 2022
Journal of Sound & Vibration (2008) · Britannica bridge engineering
Contributing: no mandatory aeroelastic testing pre-construction
Aligned failure layers — each a hole in the defence
Extreme deck slenderness (1:350 depth/span). Solid plate girders — airfoil cross-section
generating lift. Insufficient torsional stiffness. No aerodynamic pre-validation.
Hole: bridge strong statically, but aeroelastically unstable.
Cost pressure pushed design toward Moisseiff's slender deflection-theory concept.
Eldridge's conservative objections overruled. Buffers & tie-downs installed late, ineffective.
Hole: post-opening remediation was partial and too slow.
Federal and state experts approved plans under prevailing 1930s theory.
No mandatory wind-tunnel or aeroelastic testing requirement existed for long-span bridges.
Hole: profession-wide blind spot on wind-induced dynamics.
1930s suspension-bridge practice relied on deflection theory (static).
Self-excited aeroelastic flutter not yet part of standard bridge design vocabulary.
Hole: accepted theory under-estimated wind-structure interaction.
Why 1: Why did the bridge collapse?
→ Large torsional oscillation caused progressive structural breakup.
Why 2: Why did torsional flutter develop?
→ Deck switched from vertical to self-excited torsional mode at ~0.23 Hz.
Why 3: Why was flutter possible at only 42 mph?
→ Deck too torsionally weak; solid girder generated destabilising aerodynamic forces.
Why 4: Why was the deck so slender & aerodynamically poor?
→ Cost-driven design followed deflection-theory preference for light, narrow decks.
Why 5: No pre-construction aeroelastic verification was required or performed.
Bridge opens — 4th longest suspension span in world
Bouncing noted — workers call it "Galloping Gertie"
Hydraulic buffers + tie-down cables installed; ineffective
42 mph wind — vertical oscillation begins (~0.60 Hz)
Last vehicle crosses; bridge closed to traffic
Cable band slips — mode switches to torsion (~0.23 Hz)
Deck twisting 45°; one side rising 28 ft each 5 seconds
Concrete sections break off; bolts shear; suspenders snap
COLLAPSE — 600-ft centre span falls into Puget Sound
Farquharson films collapse — footage becomes iconic
New Tacoma Narrows Bridge opens — open truss stiffening
Wind-tunnel testing became standard for all long-span bridge design.
Open trusses replaced solid plate girders on replacement suspension bridges.
Bronx-Whitestone, Deer Isle, Golden Gate all retrofitted with extra stiffening.
AASHTO LRFD now requires buffeting, vortex, galloping, flutter & divergence study.
Federal bridge funding mandates 3-D wind-tunnel analysis for flexible spans.
"Static strength is not enough — dynamic wind stability is essential."
Note: The Tacoma Narrows failure is considered engineering's most consequential wind-induced collapse — it permanently transformed how bridges are designed for aerodynamic stability worldwide.

Sources: WSDOT TNB History · Encyclopedia Britannica bridge engineering · Cambridge J. Fluid Mech. (2022) · ASCE Historic Landmarks · Journal of Sound & Vibration (2008) · AASHTO LRFD Bridge Design Specifications
